annotations.label: Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: a Nash equilibrium is a situation where no player could gain more by changing their own strategy

annotations.note: Mathlib has no game-theory library and no definition of Nash equilibrium.

annotations.label: Two-player Nash equilibrium (Alice and Bob)

anchor.snippet: Alice has no other strategy available that does better than A

annotations.note: No notion of players, strategies, or best responses exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence for finite games

anchor.snippet: John Nash showed that there is a Nash equilibrium, possibly in mixed strategies

annotations.note: The Nash existence theorem for finite games is not in Mathlib (no Brouwer/Kakutani fixed-point theorem either).

annotations.label: Cournot equilibrium

anchor.snippet: A Cournot equilibrium occurs when each firm's output maximizes its profits given the output of the other firms

annotations.note: No economics/oligopoly modeling exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mixed-strategy existence for zero-sum games (von Neumann)

anchor.snippet: a mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium will exist for any zero-sum game with a finite set of actions

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Sion's generalization of the von Neumann minimax theorem (saddle-point existence on compact convex sets), but the finite-action mixed-strategy zero-sum game case is not spelled out (file's own TODO).

annotations.label: Nash existence for finite-action games (1951)

anchor.snippet: at least one (mixed-strategy) Nash equilibrium must exist in such a game

annotations.note: Nash's 1951 existence theorem for general finite games is not formalized.

annotations.label: Strategy profile

anchor.snippet: A strategy profile is a set of strategies, one for each player.

annotations.note: No definition of strategy profiles exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash equilibrium (informal)

anchor.snippet: no player can do better by unilaterally changing their strategy

annotations.note: No Nash equilibrium concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Best response characterization

anchor.snippet: each strategy in a Nash equilibrium is a best response to the other players' strategies

annotations.note: No best-response or Nash equilibrium machinery exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash equilibrium (formal)

anchor.snippet: The strategy profile

annotations.note: The formal payoff-inequality definition of Nash equilibrium is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiple Nash equilibria

anchor.snippet: A game can have more than one Nash equilibrium.

annotations.note: No game-theoretic equilibria are defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A game can have more than one Nash equilibrium.

annotations.label: Strict Nash equilibrium (formal)

anchor.snippet: It is unique and called a strict Nash equilibrium if the inequality is strict

annotations.note: No strict Nash equilibrium definition exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: It is unique and called a strict Nash equilibrium if the inequality is strict

annotations.label: Pure- vs mixed-strategy equilibrium

anchor.snippet: A game can have a pure-strategy or a mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium.

annotations.note: No pure/mixed strategy distinction exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strict Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: then the equilibrium is classified as a strict Nash equilibrium

annotations.note: No strict Nash equilibrium concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weak / non-strict Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: then the equilibrium is classified as a weak

annotations.note: No weak/non-strict equilibrium concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strong Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: a strong Nash equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium in which no coalition, taking the actions of its complements as given, can cooperatively deviate

annotations.note: No coalition/strong equilibrium concepts exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strong Nash is weakly Pareto efficient

anchor.snippet: strong Nash equilibrium has to be weakly Pareto efficient

annotations.note: Neither strong Nash equilibrium nor Pareto efficiency is formalized (Mathlib's only Pareto is the probability distribution).

annotations.label: Coalition-proof Nash equilibrium (CPNE)

anchor.snippet: occurs when players cannot do better even if they are allowed to communicate

annotations.note: No coalition-proof equilibrium concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CPNE via iterated strict dominance

anchor.snippet: Every correlated strategy supported by iterated strict dominance and on the Pareto frontier is a CPNE.

annotations.note: No iterated dominance, correlated strategies, or CPNE exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash existence theorem

anchor.snippet: every game with a finite number of players in which each player can choose from finitely many pure strategies has at least one Nash equilibrium

annotations.note: The Nash existence theorem is not formalized; Mathlib lacks the Brouwer/Kakutani fixed-point theorems it relies on.

annotations.label: Non-existence for non-compact choice sets

anchor.snippet: Nash equilibria need not exist if the set of choices is infinite and non-compact.

annotations.note: No game-theoretic existence/non-existence results exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence for compact set with continuous payoffs

anchor.snippet: a Nash equilibrium exists if the set of choices is compact with each player's payoff continuous

annotations.note: The Debreu–Fan–Glicksberg-type continuous-game existence theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized Nash equilibrium (GNE)

anchor.snippet: the equilibrium is often called Generalized Nash equilibrium (GNE)

annotations.note: No generalized Nash equilibrium with coupled constraints exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rosen's existence theorem for GNE

anchor.snippet: Rosen [ 23 ] proved that, if the set of strategy-profiles is any convex set

annotations.note: Rosen's GNE existence theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schmeidler's existence for non-atomic games

anchor.snippet: David Schmeidler [ 24 ] proved that an equilibrium exists under certain conditions.

annotations.note: Schmeidler's non-atomic-game equilibrium existence theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash equilibrium need not be Pareto optimal

anchor.snippet: a Nash equilibrium is not necessarily Pareto optimal

annotations.note: Neither Nash equilibrium nor Pareto optimality is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coordination game

anchor.snippet: The coordination game is a classic two-player, two- strategy game

annotations.note: No specific games are modeled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stag hunt

anchor.snippet: A famous example of a coordination game is the stag hunt.

annotations.note: The stag hunt game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Driving game

anchor.snippet: Driving on a road against an oncoming car

annotations.note: The driving game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Driving game mixed-strategy equilibria

anchor.snippet: there are three Nash equilibria for the same case

annotations.note: No mixed-strategy equilibrium computations exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Network traffic equilibrium

anchor.snippet: An application of Nash equilibria is in determining the expected flow of traffic in a network.

annotations.note: No traffic/network equilibrium models exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Braess's paradox

anchor.snippet: adding another possible route can decrease the efficiency of the system, a phenomenon known as Braess's paradox

annotations.note: Braess's paradox does not appear anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Competition game

anchor.snippet: This game has a unique pure-strategy Nash equilibrium: both players choosing 0

annotations.note: The competition game and its equilibrium are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Payoff matrix Nash equilibrium rule

anchor.section: Nash equilibria in a payoff matrix
anchor.snippet: if the first payoff number, in the payoff pair of the cell, is the maximum of the column of the cell

annotations.note: No payoff-matrix equilibrium criterion exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 3×3 matrix Nash equilibria

anchor.section: Nash equilibria in a payoff matrix
anchor.snippet: see that the Nash equilibria cells are (B,A), (A,B), and (C,C)

annotations.note: No worked payoff-matrix equilibrium examples exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bound on number of pure-strategy equilibria

anchor.section: Nash equilibria in a payoff matrix
anchor.snippet: An N×N matrix may have between 0 and N×N pure-strategy Nash equilibria.

annotations.note: No counting bounds for Nash equilibria exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stable Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: A Nash equilibrium for a mixed-strategy game is stable if a small change

annotations.note: No stability notion for equilibria exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Driving game stability

anchor.snippet: there are both stable and unstable equilibria

annotations.note: No equilibrium-stability analysis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mertens-stable equilibria

anchor.snippet: Mertens-stable equilibria were introduced as a solution concept

annotations.note: Mertens-stable equilibria are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sufficient conditions for NE to be played

anchor.snippet: Sufficient conditions to guarantee that the Nash equilibrium is played are:

annotations.note: No behavioral/occurrence conditions for equilibria exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Aumann–Brandenburger epistemic conditions

anchor.snippet: if the game and the rationality of players is mutually known and these conjectures are commonly known, then the conjectures must be a Nash equilibrium

annotations.note: No epistemic game theory or common-knowledge framework exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mass-action interpretation

anchor.snippet: then this stable average frequency constitutes a mixed strategy Nash equilibrium

annotations.note: The mass-action interpretation of equilibrium is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subgame perfect Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: The subgame perfect equilibrium in addition to the Nash equilibrium requires that the strategy also is a Nash equilibrium in every subgame of that game.

annotations.note: No extensive-form games or subgame perfection exist in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: The existence of a Nash equilibrium is equivalent to

annotations.note: The Kakutani fixed-point theorem is not in Mathlib (only the Riesz–Markov–Kakutani representation theorem).

annotations.label: Gain function

anchor.section: Alternate proof using the Brouwer fixed-point theorem
anchor.snippet: The gain function represents the benefit a player gets by unilaterally changing their strategy.

annotations.note: No gain function from Nash's proof is defined in Mathlib.

anchors.section: Alternate proof using the Brouwer fixed-point theorem
anchors.snippet: The gain function represents the benefit a player gets by unilaterally changing their strategy.

annotations.label: Existence proof via Brouwer fixed-point theorem

anchor.section: Alternate proof using the Brouwer fixed-point theorem
anchor.snippet: Applying the Brouwer fixed point theorem to

annotations.note: The Brouwer fixed-point theorem itself is not in Mathlib, so this proof is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Applying the Brouwer fixed point theorem to

annotations.label: Dominant strategy yields a Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: If a player A has a dominant strategy

annotations.note: No dominant-strategy notion exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strictly dominant strategies give a unique equilibrium

anchor.snippet: If both A and B have strictly dominant strategies, there exists a unique Nash equilibrium in which each plays their strictly dominant strategy.

annotations.note: No strict-dominance or uniqueness-of-equilibrium results exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Indifference condition for mixed equilibria

anchor.snippet: In order for a player to be willing to randomize, their expected payoff for each (pure) strategy should be the same.

annotations.note: The indifference principle for mixed-strategy equilibria is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matching pennies mixed equilibrium

anchor.snippet: In the matching pennies game, player A loses a point to B if A and B play the same strategy

annotations.note: The matching pennies game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Oddness theorem (Wilson)

anchor.snippet: finite games have a finite and odd number of Nash equilibria

annotations.note: Wilson's oddness theorem for generic finite games is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prisoner's dilemma and battle of the sexes equilibria

anchor.snippet: The prisoner's dilemma , for example, has one equilibrium, while the battle of the sexes has three

annotations.note: The prisoner's dilemma and battle of the sexes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free money game (even number of equilibria)

anchor.snippet: The free money game is an example of a

annotations.note: The free money game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash's 1950 existence theorem

anchor.snippet: In 1950 Nash proved that every

annotations.note: Nash's 1950 existence theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Families of games with pure-strategy NE

anchor.snippet: there are large families of games that have NE in pure strategies

annotations.note: No families of pure-strategy-solvable games are characterized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-player pure-strategy solvability and hypergraph duality

anchor.snippet: In case of two players, Nash solvability in pure strategies is equivalent to duality of hypergraphs.

annotations.note: Neither game solvability nor hypergraph duality in this sense is formalized in Mathlib.
